# Copilot Instructions

This is a Python learning companion website for high school students at MissionBit — many of whom have never written a single line of code before. All content suggestions should reflect that.

## Audience
- High school students (ages 14–18), complete beginners
- No assumed knowledge of programming, math beyond algebra, or other languages
- Students may be learning English as a second language — keep language clear and direct

## Writing style
- Write like you're explaining to a curious friend, not writing a textbook
- Use short sentences and short paragraphs
- Prefer everyday analogies over abstract definitions (e.g. "a variable is like a labeled box that stores a value")
- Avoid or define any word a student might not know
- When a technical term is unavoidable, introduce it clearly: "This is called a **loop** — it repeats a block of code"
- Do not assume the student has seen any other programming language
- Avoid phrases like "as you know", "obviously", "simply", or "just" — nothing is obvious to a beginner
- Positive, encouraging tone — mistakes are part of learning

## Code examples
- Keep examples short and focused on one concept at a time
- Use concrete, relatable scenarios (ages, names, scores, everyday objects)
- Always explain what the code does after showing it — don't leave the reader to figure it out
- Include comments in code (`# like this`) to explain non-obvious lines
- Prefer `print()` output examples so students can immediately run and verify

## Content structure
- Start with "why does this matter?" before explaining "what is it?"
- Use the existing `<GlossaryTerm>` component to link technical terms to the glossary
- Break concepts into small steps — one idea per section

## What to avoid
- Wall-of-text paragraphs
- Jargon without explanation (e.g. "instantiate", "iterable", "runtime", "compile")
- Comparisons to other languages (Java, C, JavaScript) — students don't know them
- Overly complex examples that introduce multiple new concepts at once
